era_context: The beginning of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE), a major conflict in Classical Greece. Sparta, at the height of its military power, faces a coalition of Greek city-states (Athens, Corinth, Thebes, Argos) funded by Persian gold. This war marks a turning point: Spartan hegemony begins to decline, and the balance of power in Greece shifts. In the Near East, Artaxerxes II is successfully using Greek factional conflicts to prevent any single power from threatening Persian interests. The broader Mediterranean continues to see Greek expansion and trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of Persian Empire beyond court records and Greek accounts

events.title: Outbreak of the Corinthian War
events.description: The Corinthian War begins when Corinth and Thebes, supported by Persian gold and encouraged by Athens, attack Spartan allies. The immediate cause is a dispute over Corinthian territory, but the underlying cause is Persian strategy to contain Spartan power by funding anti-Spartan coalitions. The war involves major battles and naval engagements over the next twelve years. Sparta, despite its military superiority, finds itself fighting on multiple fronts and unable to achieve decisive victory. The war marks the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece.
key_figures: Lysander (Spartan general, recently deceased but his legacy influential)
events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the Corinthian War in 395 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Xenophon provides a detailed account of the war's beginning. The date 395 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence.
cross_references: Follows from Persian strategy to contain Sparta (-397 to -396)

events.title: Battle of Haliartus and Spartan setback
events.description: Early in the Corinthian War, Spartan forces under Agesilaus II engage Theban and Athenian forces at Haliartus in Boeotia. The battle is tactically indecisive but strategically significant: Spartan forces are unable to achieve a decisive victory, and the coalition forces demonstrate that they can stand against Spartan hoplites. The battle sets the tone for the war: despite Spartan military superiority, the coalition's superior numbers and Persian financial support enable them to sustain the conflict.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Haliartus is attested in Xenophon and other ancient sources. The date 395 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship. However, the exact tactical outcome and significance of the battle are debated by modern scholars.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (-395 to -387)
Demonstrates the coalition's ability to resist Spartan military dominance

events.title: Conon's naval operations and Persian support
events.description: Conon, commanding a Persian-funded naval force, continues operations against Spartan naval dominance. His fleet is being built up with Persian resources to challenge Spartan control of the Aegean. These naval operations are part of the broader Persian strategy to contain Sparta and support the anti-Spartan coalition. Conon's naval activities will culminate in the Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE), where he will achieve a major victory over the Spartan fleet.
events.certainty_note: Conon's naval operations in 395 BCE are attested in Xenophon and later sources. However, the precise dating of specific naval engagements is uncertain. Ancient sources sometimes compress events across multiple years.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (-395 to -387)

disconfirming_evidence: The causes of the Corinthian War are debated by modern scholars. While Persian gold clearly supported anti-Spartan coalitions, the degree to which Persia actively engineered the war versus opportunistically exploited existing Greek tensions is disputed. Some scholars emphasize the role of local Greek grievances and factional conflicts, while others stress Persian agency. Additionally, the extent of Spartan military superiority is debated: while Sparta won many battles, it was unable to achieve decisive victory, suggesting that the coalition's superior numbers and resources were more significant than traditional accounts suggest. The Battle of Haliartus is sometimes described as a Spartan victory, while other sources describe it as indecisive or even a coalition success.
historiographic_note: Sources for 395 BCE are more abundant than for the preceding years, primarily because the Corinthian War is a major event in Classical Greek history. Xenophon's Hellenica is the primary narrative source, and it provides detailed accounts of military campaigns and battles. However, Xenophon was a Spartan sympathizer, and his account may reflect bias in favor of Spartan actions. Persian sources are largely absent from the surviving record, though inscriptions and later Greek accounts of Persian actions exist. The year 395 BCE marks a turning point in Greek history: the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony and the rise of Persian influence in Greek affairs. The historical record for this year is relatively rich compared to the preceding years, but it is still heavily weighted toward Greek (especially Athenian and Spartan) perspectives.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War continues with major naval and land battles
Battle of Haliartus is part of the broader Corinthian War
Conon's naval operations culminate in the Battle of Cnidus
The Corinthian War leads to the Peace of Antalcidas
